Because I’ve been through a lot of change, moving to this country and seeing how things work, I’ve learned that the most important thing is to keep your head steady, even when things are loud. I spend a lot of time on the internet, seeing all these different views, and it can be overwhelming, making my stomach feel tight, you know?  For me, the trick is not to try and win the argument, because that just brings more trouble and stress. Instead, I try to focus on what I know to be true, which is that every person has their own path and their own beliefs, just like different ways of traveling or different places I’ve seen when I travel. If someone is pushing a strong view, I listen with my ears, but I don't have to agree with my heart. I try to gently steer the conversation back to facts, the real, solid information, but I do it without being aggressive; that low agreeableness of mine means I can get sharp, but I don't want that noise. If the discussion starts to get too heated, too much yelling or blaming, I just step away. I put my phone down, maybe go for a short cycle ride if I can, or just sit quietly for a bit. It's about protecting my peace of mind, because frankly, all this worry is not good for my health. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When these big talks about health and those vaccines start up, especially online where everyone seems so sure of themselves, it can feel like a storm brewing. Because I’ve been through a lot of change, moving to this country and seeing how things work, I’ve learned that the most important thing is to keep your head steady, even when things are loud. I spend a lot of time on the internet, seeing all these different views, and it can be overwhelming, making my stomach feel tight, you know?  For me, the trick is not to try and win the argument, because that just brings more trouble and stress. Instead, I try to focus on what I know to be true, which is that every person has their own path and their own beliefs, just like different ways of traveling or different places I’ve seen when I travel. If someone is pushing a strong view, I listen with my ears, but I don't have to agree with my heart. I try to gently steer the conversation back to facts, the real, solid information, but I do it without being aggressive; that low agreeableness of mine means I can get sharp, but I don't want that noise. If the discussion starts to get too heated, too much yelling or blaming, I just step away. I put my phone down, maybe go for a short cycle ride if I can, or just sit quietly for a bit. It's about protecting my peace of mind, because frankly, all this worry is not good for my health. I respect that people feel strongly, but I hold onto my own careful understanding, the one I build slowly, piece by piece, rather than reacting to the rush of emotion online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 80-year-old immigrant with primary education, high neuroticism, and low agreeableness. The tone should be cautious, somewhat anxious but highly self-controlled (conscientiousness), and perhaps slightly world-weary. The advice needs to be practical for managing online stress (given high social media time and high neuroticism) while reflecting a pragmatic, non-confrontational approach (despite low agreeableness, the high neuroticism necessitates de-escalation). The language should be simple, reflective of primary education.
